steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The blank follows "With over two millennia of climate data in its tree ______" and precedes "single tree like this, claims dendrochronologist Valerie Trouet, can tell the history of the world." The blank must (1) close the introductory prepositional phrase ending in "rings" and (2) introduce the subject of the main clause with the right article. The main verb is "can tell," so the subject must be a noun phrase — "a single tree like this."

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — rings, a: A comma correctly separates the introductory phrase ("With over two millennia of climate data in its tree rings") from the main clause. "a" supplies the article needed to start the subject "a single tree like this." The parenthetical "claims dendrochronologist Valerie Trouet" is properly set off by commas within the main clause. ✓
  • Choice B — rings. A: A period creates a sentence fragment: "With over two millennia of climate data in its tree rings." has no subject or main verb — it cannot stand alone as a sentence. ✗
  • Choice C — rings and a: "And" is a coordinating conjunction that joins two independent clauses, but "With over two millennia of climate data in its tree rings" is a prepositional phrase, not an independent clause. This creates an illogical compound structure. ✗
  • Choice D — rings; a: A semicolon must connect two independent clauses, but the phrase before the blank is not an independent clause — it has no subject or verb. Using a semicolon here is a punctuation error. ✗
A is correct.
